questions.explanation: "last year" is a finished time, so we use the past simple: I went to Paris last year. The present perfect (have gone/been) cannot take a finished-time marker, and "has gone" is the wrong subject form for I.

options.text: She has just left the office.
They have lived here for years.

questions.explanation: The error is (a): "yesterday" is a finished time, so it needs the past simple — I saw him yesterday, not "have seen". (b), (c) and (d) all use the present perfect correctly (just, for years, yet).

questions.prompt: Complete: "She has ___ three novels so far."
questions.explanation: write is irregular: write → wrote (past) → written (participle). After has we use the participle: She has written. "wrote" is the past simple, "writed" is not a word, and "write" is the base form.

"since 2018" describes a period from the past up to now, which takes the present perfect: We have lived here since 2018. "lived" (past simple) and "had lived" (past perfect) cut the link to now, and "live" is the present simple.

questions.prompt: You have been to Egypt. Choose the correct short answer to "Have you ever been to Egypt?"
questions.explanation: A present-perfect question is answered with the same auxiliary: Have you…? — Yes, I have. "I did" answers a past-simple question, and "I am / I was" answer questions with the verb to be, not the present perfect.

options.text: She has gone to the shop, but she's back now.
He has been to Japan twice.
They have gone home; they left an hour ago.
I have been very busy this week.

questions.explanation: The error is (a): "has gone" means she is still away, which contradicts "she's back now" — it should be has been (visited and returned). (b), (c) and (d) use been/gone correctly for their meanings.
